title: Untrusted peer DataChannel payload passed to handlers with zero validation
detail: conn.on('data', (data) => { ... const message = data as MultiplayerMessage; this.handleMessage(message); }). The incoming WebRTC data from an untrusted peer is type-asserted (`as MultiplayerMessage`) and dispatched directly. The empty catch only guards a synchronous throw that never happens here. By contrast WebSocketAdapter.handleMessage validates via isValidWSMessage() before use. A malicious peer can send `{type:'PLAYER_JOIN', payload:{...}}` with arbitrary id/name/color, or any message with a missing/malformed payload, and it flows straight into MultiplayerManager.handleMessage -> Zustand store mutations (store.addRemotePlayer, store.setMachineLock, store.resolveIntent, store.addChatMessage). message.payload.timestamp in the PING handler is dereferenced unchecked (line 79) - a PING with no payload throws and is silently swallowed, but the real risk is the unvalidated objects reaching store mutators.
fix: Add a validateMultiplayerMessage(data): data is MultiplayerMessage type-guard (mirroring messageValidation.ts/isValidWSMessage): check data is an object, type is one of the known string literals, and the payload shape matches per-type (e.g. PLAYER_JOIN requires id:string,name:string,color in PLAYER_COLORS). Reject (return without dispatch, increment an error counter) on failure. Wire it at the top of PeerConnection.handleMessage before any branch.
_area: L2 Security · Multiplayer peer trust + SCADA adapter security

title: Host trusts client-supplied playerId in INTENT (player impersonation / authority bypass)
detail: case 'INTENT': const result = this.onMachineIntent(message.payload). The host executes message.payload (a MachineIntent) whose playerId field is fully attacker-controlled by the sending peer. Nothing binds the intent's playerId to the authenticated `peerId` of the connection it arrived on. A malicious guest can submit intents tagged with another player's id (or the host's id), defeating any per-player attribution, machine-lock ownership, or rate logic in the intent handler. Same trust gap exists in handlePeerDisconnected (line 252-255) which removes a player based on connection metadata.playerId, and in handleMessage PLAYER_LEAVE (line 302-303) where any peer can send PLAYER_LEAVE with an arbitrary id to evict another player from the host's store.
fix: On the host, override the trusted identity: build the effective intent from the connection's authenticated peerId rather than the payload, e.g. const trustedPlayerId = this.peerIdToPlayerId.get(peerId); this.onMachineIntent({...message.payload, playerId: trustedPlayerId}). For PLAYER_LEAVE, ignore the payload id when isHost and use the peerId mapping. Maintain a peerId->playerId map populated from the verified connection at join time.
_area: L2 Security · Multiplayer peer trust + SCADA adapter security

title: PLAYER_UPDATE/PLAYER_JOIN let any peer mutate arbitrary remote-player entries by id
detail: case 'PLAYER_UPDATE': store.updateRemotePlayer(message.payload.id, message.payload). The id is taken from the untrusted payload, not from the authenticated peerId. A peer can send PLAYER_UPDATE with id set to another player's id and overwrite their position/name/color/selectedMachineId, or PLAYER_JOIN (line 288-300) to inject phantom players. There is no check that payload.id === the sending connection's player. In a host-authority model only the host should originate PLAYER_JOIN/UPDATE about other players; a guest should only be able to update its own entry.
fix: When isHost, derive the player id from the connection's peerId map and ignore payload.id for PLAYER_UPDATE from guests; only accept updates where payload.id maps to that peer. Treat PLAYER_JOIN/PLAYER_LEAVE as host-authoritative: guests must not be able to inject them (drop these message types when received from a non-host peer).
_area: L2 Security · Multiplayer peer trust + SCADA adapter security

title: No inbound message-size or rate bound on WebRTC DataChannel (untrusted-peer DoS)
detail: PeerConnection has no cap on the size or frequency of incoming messages. A malicious peer can flood the host with FULL_STATE_SYNC/CHAT/PLAYER_UPDATE messages or a single huge payload (e.g. CHAT.message of many MB, or a STATE_SYNC with a giant machines array), all of which are dispatched into Zustand store mutations and, for CHAT, accumulated in addChatMessage. There is no per-peer message-rate throttle or byte-length guard. Combined with the validation gap above, this is a cheap client-side DoS / memory-exhaustion vector.
fix: In PeerConnection.handleMessage, reject messages whose serialized JSON length exceeds a sane cap (e.g. 64KB for control msgs); enforce a max CHAT.message length (e.g. 500 chars) in validation; add a simple per-peer token-bucket rate limit (drop+count when exceeded). Cap chat history length in store.addChatMessage.
_area: L2 Security · Multiplayer peer trust + SCADA adapter security

title: Room codes are low-entropy and brute-forceable via PeerJS public server
detail: generateRoomCode() draws 6 chars from a 32-char alphabet = ~30 bits, but the room peer-id is the predictable string `millos-${roomCode}` (SignalingService.ts:54/117). Because the host registers a guessable, namespaced peer-id on the shared PeerJS *public* cloud server, an attacker can enumerate `millos-XXXXXX` ids to discover/join active rooms (or probe which codes are live) without any invite, room password, or host approval. There is no admission control: handleIncomingConnection accepts every connection and immediately adds the peer (SignalingService.ts:134-137, MultiplayerManager.ts:136). Combined with the unvalidated-payload and intent-spoof issues, an unsolicited peer who guesses a code gets full guest authority.
fix: Add an admission step: host should require a join secret (the host-displayed code can stay short, but gate actual acceptance on a longer shared token passed in conn.metadata and verified before calling onPeerConnected/addPeer). Reject connections whose metadata token does not match. Longer term, self-host peerjs-server (already noted as a TODO in SignalingService PEERJS_CONFIG comment) and randomize the host peer-id instead of deriving it from the room code.
_area: L2 Security · Multiplayer peer trust + SCADA adapter security

title: Wonderware adapter forces plaintext HTTP, sending Basic-auth credentials in cleartext
detail: this.baseUrl = `http://${this.serverHost}:${this.serverPort}/Historian/v1`; is hardcoded to http:// (never https). The constructor then builds `Basic ${btoa(domain\\username:password)}` (lines 95-102) and fetchWithAuth attaches it as the Authorization header (lines 314-328). Base64 is encoding, not encryption: any on-path observer reading the cleartext HTTP request recovers the Windows/SQL domain credentials verbatim. There is no scheme option and no upgrade to TLS. This is a credential-disclosure / SCADA-network downgrade exposure.
fix: Default the scheme to https:// and make it configurable (e.g. add a `secure`/`scheme` field to WonderwareConnectionConfig, defaulting to https). Refuse to send Basic-auth credentials over a plain-http URL (throw, or warn-and-strip) unless the host is explicitly localhost. Mirror the same guard in any other adapter that builds a base URL.
_area: L2 Security · Multiplayer peer trust + SCADA adapter security

title: Bearer token attached to any URL with no scheme/host allowlist (token leak to untrusted endpoints)
detail: fetchWithAuth validates only `new URL(url)` parses (line 346), then unconditionally sets `Authorization: Bearer ${this.config.apiKey}` (line 354) for every request, including to whatever baseUrl the user/config supplies. There is no check that the scheme is https or that the host matches the configured baseUrl origin. If baseUrl is http:// (allowed - no scheme restriction), the bearer token is sent in cleartext. The URL-validation comment implies a security control but it only guards against malformed URLs, not against sending credentials to an attacker-controlled or downgraded endpoint. PIAdapter.fetchWithAuth (PIAdapter.ts:338-360) has the same pattern with no URL/scheme validation at all.
fix: In fetchWithAuth, only attach the Authorization header when url.protocol === 'https:' (or host is localhost), and assert the request URL's origin matches the configured baseUrl origin before sending the token. Apply the same origin/scheme guard in PIAdapter.fetchWithAuth.
_area: L2 Security · Multiplayer peer trust + SCADA adapter security

title: WebSocket adapter silently downgrades https->ws (insecure) instead of wss
detail: const url = wsUrl.replace(/^http/, 'ws'); maps both http->ws AND https->ws? No: it maps https->wss correctly only by accident because 'https' starts with 'http' and becomes 'wsss'... actually `'https://x'.replace(/^http/,'ws')` yields 'wss://x' (correct). But a bare `ws://`/`http://` config is accepted with no enforcement of a secure scheme, so a plaintext, unauthenticated WebSocket to a SCADA proxy is allowed. The bigger gap: there is no auth on the WS connection at all (no token in the URL or first message) - any party who can reach the proxy URL can subscribe to and write tags. Writes are sent as `{type:'write', tagId, value}` (lines 216-220) over this unauthenticated channel.
fix: Warn or refuse when the resolved url is ws:// (non-wss) unless host is localhost. Add an auth token (from config) to the WS handshake (query param or first 'subscribe' message) so the proxy can reject unauthenticated clients. Confirm the https->wss replacement intent with an explicit scheme map rather than a regex on 'http'.
_area: L2 Security · Multiplayer peer trust + SCADA adapter security

title: Validated WS message tags written to value store with no tagId allowlist (unvalidated tag writes / injection)
detail: In the 'batch'/'snapshot' branch, msg.tags is iterated and `this.values.set(tv.tagId, tv)` stores every tag id the server (or a MITM on an unauthenticated ws://) sends, even tag ids not in this.tags (the known TagDefinition set). isValidWSMessage only checks tagId is a non-empty string, not that it is a known tag. A malicious/compromised proxy can inject arbitrary tagIds and values that then propagate to global subscribers and any UI/alarm logic keyed on tag values. The 'update' branch (line 311-317) has the same gap. Writes outbound are gated by accessMode (line 211) but inbound values are not gated to known tags.
fix: In handleMessage update/batch/snapshot branches, drop tag values whose tagId is not in this.tags (or whose accessMode forbids the source). i.e. `if (!this.tags.has(t.tagId)) return;` before this.values.set. Optionally clamp numeric values to the tag's engLow/engHigh.
_area: L2 Security · Multiplayer peer trust + SCADA adapter security

title: CHAT message stored from untrusted payload with attacker-controlled 'from'/'fromName' and no length bound
detail: case 'CHAT': store.addChatMessage(message.payload). The entire ChatMessage (id, from, fromName, message, timestamp) is taken from the untrusted peer with no validation. from/fromName can be spoofed to impersonate another player or the host in the chat UI, and message has no length cap (DoS / store bloat). Rendering is via React JSX (MultiplayerChat.tsx auto-escapes, so no DOM XSS), but identity spoofing in chat and unbounded growth remain.
fix: Validate CHAT payload shape and cap message length (e.g. 500 chars). On the host, overwrite from/fromName with the sender's authenticated peer identity before re-broadcasting, so guests cannot forge sender identity.
_area: L2 Security · Multiplayer peer trust + SCADA adapter security

title: Guest accepts FULL_STATE_SYNC/STATE_SYNC from any connected peer, not verified-as-host
detail: case 'FULL_STATE_SYNC' / 'STATE_SYNC' only check `!store.isHost` before applying the payload to the production store (applyFullState/applyStateDiff dispatch window CustomEvents that drive machine state). They do not verify the message actually came from the host connection. In the current topology a guest connects only to the host, but PeerJS allows any peer to open a DataConnection to the guest's peer-id (`millos-${roomCode}-${playerId}`, predictable). Such a peer could push a forged FULL_STATE_SYNC to overwrite the guest's machine state, weather, and machine locks.
fix: Record the host's peerId on join and, in these branches, verify the originating peerId matches the known host before applying. Drop state-sync messages from any non-host peer.
_area: L2 Security · Multiplayer peer trust + SCADA adapter security

title: PI Basic-auth credentials base64-encoded with no TLS enforcement on baseUrl
detail: case 'basic': const credentials = btoa(`${config.username}:${config.password}`); this.authHeader = `Basic ${credentials}`. The baseUrl is used verbatim in fetchWithAuth with no scheme check (PIAdapter.ts:338-360, no `new URL()` validation at all, unlike RESTAdapter). If a user configures an http:// PI Web API baseUrl, the Basic credentials are transmitted in cleartext. The comment at line 354 ('SSL verification is browser-controlled') acknowledges TLS is not enforced by this code.
fix: Before sending Authorization, assert this.baseUrl uses https:// (or host is localhost); otherwise throw a clear configuration error. Add `new URL()` validation to PIAdapter.fetchWithAuth to match RESTAdapter's guard.
_area: L2 Security · Multiplayer peer trust + SCADA adapter security

title: Host peer-id squatting: predictable id enables room hijack/denial before host registers
detail: Host peer-id is the deterministic `millos-${roomCode}` (line 54). An attacker who learns/guesses a code can register that exact peer-id on the shared public PeerJS server first, causing the legitimate host's Peer() to fail with 'unavailable-id' (handled at line 82) OR letting the attacker impersonate the host to guests that connect to `millos-${roomCode}`. There is no proof-of-host (signature/secret) binding the room to the legitimate creator.
fix: Bind room ownership to a secret: host generates a random host token, derives a non-guessable peer-id (e.g. include a high-entropy suffix not shown to guests until they prove the join code), and guests verify a host-signed value in metadata. Minimum: self-host the signaling server so id allocation can be authenticated.
_area: L2 Security · Multiplayer peer trust + SCADA adapter security

title: Connection metadata trusted for playerId without validation (disconnect/eviction targeting)
detail: getMetadata() returns the raw conn.metadata cast to Record<string,unknown>. MultiplayerManager.handlePeerDisconnected (line 251-255) reads metadata.playerId and uses it (when host) as the player id to removeRemotePlayer/removePeer and broadcast PLAYER_LEAVE. metadata is fully controlled by the connecting peer (set client-side in SignalingService.connectToHost metadata, lines 122-125). A guest can set metadata.playerId to another player's id so that, on its own disconnect, the host evicts the victim instead.
fix: On the host, map peerId->playerId at verified join time and use that map for disconnect cleanup; never trust metadata.playerId for destructive store operations. Treat metadata as a display hint only.
_area: L2 Security · Multiplayer peer trust + SCADA adapter security
